coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon dots as a novel fluorescent probe for fluorogenic sensing of Ce4+ and ratiometric detection of Al3+
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xin
Zhao, Liuxi
Wu, Yuhan
Zhou, Ao
Jiang, Xuanfeng
Zhan, Yuan
Sun, Zhengguang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots have been widely focused on the field of metal ion detection due to their excellent optical property. Herein, novel orange fluorescent nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon dots (NB-CDs) are obtained by one-pot solvothermal using p-phenylenediamine and boric acid as raw materials. The NB-CDs exhibit excitation-independent emissions and the maximum emission wavelength is 597 nm at 420 nm excitation. The fluorescence can be quenched by Ce4+ effectively and selectively, and the detection range of Ce4+ is gained from 0.14 to 180 μM with a detection limit of as low as 0.14 μM. Furthermore, Al3+ can also recombine with NB-CDs surface functional groups, which shows a detection range from 1.07 to 100 μM and a detection limit of as low as 1.07 μM, accompanied with a blue-shift to 527 nm.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Boron
Co-doped carbon dots
Fluorescence detection
Ce4+
Al3+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon dots (NB-CDs)
Fluorescent detection of Ce4+ ions based on NB-CDs
Ratio fluorescence detection of Al3+ based on NB-CDs
Application in the detection of Ce4+ and Al3+ in real sample

Hubei Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM image (a) and particle size distribution (b) of NB-CDs.
(a) FT-IR spectrum, (b) XPS spectrum, high-resolution XPS spectra of (c) C1s, (d) N1s, (e) O1s and (f) B1s of the NB-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption, PL excitation, and emission spectra of the NB-CDs. Inset picture is the NB-CDs under chamber light and 365 nm UV light, respectively. (b) fluorescence spectra of NB-CDs at different excitation wavelengths.

(a) Fluorescence response of NB-CDs in the presence of Ce4+. Ce4+ concentration, from top to bottom: 0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 120, 140, 160, 180, 200, 220, 240, 260, 280, 300, 320, 340, 360, 380, 400 μM. (b) the linear relationship between F0 / F and the concentrations of Ce4+ in the range of 0–180 μM, where F0 and F represent the PL intensity of the NB-CDs in the absence and presence of Ce4+. (c) and (d) Images of NB-CDs in the presence of Ce4+ (from left to right, 0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 140, 160, 180 μM) under chamber light and ultraviolet light (Ex. = 365 nm), respectively.

(a) Fluorescence response of NB-CDs in the presence of Al3+. Al3+ concentration, from top to bottom: 0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 120, 140, 160, 180, 200, 220, 240, 260, 280, 300, 320, 340, 360, 380, 400 μM. (b) and (c) The plot of F527nm / F597nm as a function of the Al3+ concentration, the inset showing the linear range of 0–100 μM for Al3+. (d) and (e) Images of NB-CDs in the presence of Al3+ (from left to right, 0, 100, 200, 300, 400 μM) under chamber light and ultraviolet light (Ex. = 365 nm), respectively.

(a) Fluorescence response of N-CDs in the presence of Ce4+ with the concentrations from 0 to 400 μM. (b) the linear relationship between F0 / F and the concentrations of Ce4+ in the range of 0–120 μM, where F0 and F represent the PL intensity of the N-CDs in the absence and presence of Ce4+. (c) Fluorescence response of N-CDs in the presence of Al3+ with the concentrations from 0 to 400 μM. (d) The plot of F0 / F as a function of the Al3+ concentration, where F0 and F represent the PL intensity of the N-CDs in the absence and presence of Al3+.

(a) Fluorescence response of NB-CDs ethanol solution in the presence of 400 μM metal ions. (b) Effect of Ce4+ culture time on fluorescence intensity ratio (F / F0) of NB-CDs nanosensor in the range of 0–35 min at concentration of 400 μmol/L. (c) Effect of Al3+ culture time on fluorescence intensity ratio (F527nm / F597nm) of NB-CDs nanosensor in the range of 0–35 min at concentration of 400 μmol/L.
Schematic illustration of the synthetic process of NB-CDs and their function detection of Ce4+ and Al3+.
Comparison with other probes for the determination of Ce4+.

Fe2+ hybrid sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison with CDs for the determination of Al3+.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon dots as a novel fluorescent probe for fluorogenic sensing of Ce4+ and ratiometric detection of Al3+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials, Ministry of Education, Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials
Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials, Ministry of Education, Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel orange emissive nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon dots (NB-CDs) were prepared by a one-pot solvothermal method with the p-phenylenediamine and boric acid as raw material. The doping of N and B is designed to improve the fluorescence performance of CDs. The fluorescence can be effectively and selectively quenched by Ce4+ and the detection range of Ce4+ is obtained from 0.14 to 180 μM with a detection limit as low as 0.14 μM. Furthermore, Al3+ can also recombine with NB-CDs surface functional groups, which shows a detection range from 1.07 to 100 μM and a detection limit of as low as 1.07 μM, accompanied with a blue-shift to 527 nm.

Carbon dots have been widely focused on the field of metal ion detection due to their excellent optical property. Herein, novel orange fluorescent nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon dots (NB-CDs) are obtained by one-pot solvothermal using p-phenylenediamine and boric acid as raw materials. The NB-CDs exhibit excitation-independent emissions and the maximum emission wavelength is 597 nm at 420 nm excitation. The fluorescence can be quenched by Ce4+ effectively and selectively, and the detection range of Ce4+ is gained from 0.14 to 180 μM with a detection limit of as low as 0.14 μM. Furthermore, Al3+ can also recombine with NB-CDs surface functional groups, which shows a detection range from 1.07 to 100 μM and a detection limit of as low as 1.07 μM, accompanied with a blue-shift to 527 nm.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) as novel emerging extraordinarily bright fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, have attracted intensive research interest due to their facile synthesis, low toxicity, stable photoluminescence (PL), and environmental friendliness [1,2]. These characteristics make CDs show application prospect in many fields, such as bioimaging, catalysis, sensor, and so on [3,4]. In particular, CDs possess a lot of active functionality, such as C=O, –OH, –OR, –COOH etc. on their surface [5,6], which can make the fluorescence of CDs quenched or enhanced by interacting with metal ions, including photo-induced electron transfer (PET), light-induced electron transfer (LET), inner filter effect (IFE), coordination/complexation, ion binding and/or aggregation [7–9]. However, CDs still face serious challenges [10–13], especially their relatively poor probe sensitivity and selectivity [14,15]. Heteroatomic doping has become an effective strategy to improve the fluorescence properties of CDs [16–20]. One of the interesting and unique features of heteroatom doped CDs is their sensitivity, selectivity and response to molecular compounds and ions in solution, which can change the optical behaviour (fluorescence quenching or enhancement) of the CDs. For example, Wang et al. fabricated blue emissive nitrogen and boron doped carbon nanodots for NO2 − detection, in which the presence of the hetero atoms changes the electronic states of the CDs, resulting in more surface defects and an increase in fluorescence emission intensity [21]. Thus, doping heteroatoms can change the electronic characteristics of CDs, resulting in more active sites, which could produce new and unanticipated properties [22,23]. However, most of the present CDs for the metal ion detection are limited in blue emission, long-wavelength emissive CDs for the metal ion detection are very scarce.

Cerium (Ce) is an important rare earth element, and its ions are mainly Ce4+ [24,25]. Ce4+ has good antibacterial function, but its accumulation can seriously interfere with damage cell membranes, nuclei, organelles, and wreak havoc on biological systems [26]. At present, the main detection methods of Ce4+ include chromatography and electrochemical method, atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), gravimetric method, X-ray fluorescence spectrometry (XRF), but these methods have some defects such as high cost, inconvenient use, large sample consumption, which are not conducive to the wide application of detection [27]. CDs have received the attention to detect Ce4+ because of their unique property for their strong complexation with the surface functional group [28,29]. Xia et al. proposed a “turn-off” fluorescence method through graphene quantum dots (GQDs) integrated with simulated oxidase activity of Ce4+ for Ce4+ detection with fluorescent at 562 nm decreases, and the detection limit is 1.0 μM [30]. Han et al. achieved the sensing of Ce4+ in the range of 0.5–100 μM and 1.9–80 μM, through restoring the fluorescence of CDs based on the oxidation of Ce4+, with detection limits of 0.5 and 1.9 μM, respectively [31]. Chu et al. prepared N-GQDs using graphene oxide with detection range of 1–44 mM and the detection limit of 0.8352 mM for Ce4+ [29]. However, most of the reported methods are based on further functional improvements CDs through indirect means. What’s more, these methods have the limitation of slightly lower sensor sensitivity, shorter emission wavelength included.

In addition to Ce4+, excessive accumulation of Al3+ can also cause serious damage to the human body, so it is vital to develop a rapid detection method for Al3+ [32,33]. Rao et al. reported a class of biomass-based CDs whose emission at 470 nm is enhanced by Al3+ [34]. Yan et al. prepared CDs functionalized with amino acids for selective detection of Al3+, with emission at 477 nm enhanced [35]. Yao et al. synthesized blue GQDs with the emission at 454 nm through microwave-assisted single-step closed-loop condensation of glucose molecules, which responded to enhanced Al3+ fluorescence [36]. The mechanism of above detections may be that the complex formed between Al3+ and CDs limits the PET process of CDs itself. However, most of these CDs that detect Al3+ are also limited to shorter wavelengths of blue emission, which can cause eye strain and retinal damage from prolonged exposure to blue light [37,38]. After our investigation, there is still a gap in the development of fluorescence sensors that can effectively detect and recognize Ce4+ and Al3+, and it has a broad application prospect in the fields of sensing and bioimaging.

Herein, we report novel orange emissive nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon dots (NB-CDs) prepared by a one-pot solvothermal method with the p-phenylenediamine and boric acid as raw material. The prepared NB-CDs exhibit high selectivity and sensitivity of Ce4+ and Al3+ in ethanol (Scheme 1 ). The NB-CDs system emits orange fluorescence at 597 nm under 420 nm excitation. When the Ce4+ is added to the above system, the fluorescence at 597 nm decreases and the wavelength shifts slightly blue. In addition, with the increase of Al3+ concentration, the emission at 597 nm decreases and the wavelength blue shifts to 527 nm. Furthermore, the value of F527nm / F597nm gradually increases, and the fluorescence color changes from orange to green. This fluorescent probe can avoid biological tissue damage caused by the often using the blue emission and ultraviolet excitation, which would have a broad application prospect in the field of sensing [39,40].

p-Phenylenediamine (p-PD, ≥97%), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, ≥99.9%), dichloromethane (DCM, ≥99.5%), ethanol (EtOH, ≥99.7%), tetrahydrofuran (THF, ≥99.5%), and toluene (MB, ≥99.8%) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Boric acid (BA, ≥99.5%) was provided by Tianjin Chemical Reagent Factory. Petroleum ether purchased from Tianjin Fuyu company. Deionized water (D.I. water) was collected from a Milli-Q system (Millipore, U.S.A., 18.2 MΩ resistivities).

The UV-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum was obtained on a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrophotometer. The fluorescence emission spectrum was determined by a Pico-quant FT300 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The quantum yield (QY) was measured by integrating sphere fluorescence spectrophotometer, and fluorescence attenuation curve was measured by 420 nm laser fluorescence spectrophotometer. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were performed on a JEOL JEM-2100 microscope under 60 kV accelerated voltage. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was achieved by a NICOLET iS50 spectrometer using KBr pellets with a resolution of 4 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was recorded by an Escalab 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Escalab 250Xi, Thermo, America) excited by a single X-ray source Al Kα (1486.6 eV). The measurement scan point size is 500 µm × 500 µm and the passing energy is 120.0 eV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon dots (NB-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 1 mmol of p-PD and 5 mmol of BA were added in 20 mL EtOH and stirred for 5 min to make the solution evenly mixed. The solution was subsequently heated in a stainless-steel autoclave lined with Teflon at 200 °C for 10 h. Then the crude product was evenly mixed at petroleum ether with a volume ratio of 1:5, and the mixture was centrifuged at 4000 r/min for 10 min to discard the precipitate. Finally, powder of NB-CDs was obtained by drying collected supernatant in the oven at 60 °C.

The NB-CDs powder was dispersed in EtOH with the concentration of 0.2 mg/mL. For detection of Ce4+, 400 μL of Ce4+ aqueous solutions with the concentrations range of 0–400 μM were added into 3 mL NB-CDs ethanol solution. After incubation for 20 min at room temperature, fluorescence measurements were performed with 420 nm excitation. The detection of Al3+ was carried out in the same way. The limit of detection (LOD) is the three times standard deviation of the blank sample divided by the calculated slope of the linear fitting curve (3σ/s), in which we selected at least three blank samples for calculation to ensure scientific experimental data. To evaluate the selectivity of NB-CDs, 400 μM of other metal ions (Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, K+, Li+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Hg2+) on fluorescence intensity were investigated by the same method. All the fluorescence intensities were measured under the same conditions: room temperature, the concentration of NB-CDs is 0.2 mg/mL, incubation time is 20 min, excitation wavelength is at 420 nm and both emission and excitation slit are 10 nm.

Firstly, the size distribution and morphology of NB-CDs were studied by HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a, NB-CDs are evenly distributed and approximately spherical. The particle size of NB-CDs particles is concentrated between 1.43∼2.44 nm, with an average particle size of 2.05 nm (Fig. 1b) [41,42].

The chemical bonds and structure of NB-CDs were explored by FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2 a). The absorption peak at 3418 cm−1 is assigned to -OH/N-H stretching vibrations. The stretching vibration of C=O is centered at 1608 cm−1. The peak at 1393 cm−1 corresponds to C-N. The peaks at 1168 cm−1 and 1445 cm−1 are the bending vibration of B-OH bond and the stretching vibration of B-O-B bond, respectively, which suggest the presence of B element in the NB-CDs [43,44]. The absorption peak located at 1355 cm−1 is attributed to N-B, indicating that boric acid is introduced into the surface of NB-CDs carrier through chemical interaction [45,46].

The elemental composition and chemical states of NB-CDs were further studied by XPS. The full-scan spectrum (Fig. 2b) results of XPS shows four peaks at 283.89, 398.23, 531.13 and 191.25 eV, proving the presence of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and B 1s, respectively, and their contents are 56.99%, 28.26%, 9.75% and 5.00%, respectively. The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) is divided into two peaks at 284.71 eV and 285.81 eV, which are attributed to C=C/C-C, C-N/C-O [47,48]. Fig. 2d displays that the high-resolved N1s spectra at 399.36 eV, 400.48 eV and 401.50 eV are attributed to C-N, B-N [49,50] and N-H. The O1s can be deconvoluted into two peaks, which are attributed to C-OH/C-O-C (532.2 eV) and C=O (533.56 eV) (Fig. 2e). The B 1s spectrum (Fig. 2f) is deconvolved into two peaks with one peak at 192.61 eV for B-N, and other peak at 194.21 eV for B-O bond [51]. The above results indicate that both FT-IR and XPS characterization methods prove that N and B have been doped into the structure of NB-CDs successfully.

The optical characteristics of the NB-CDs were further investigated by UV–vis absorption and PL spectra. Fig. 3 a shows two distinct absorption peaks at 245 and 285 nm, which are derived from the π-π* transition [51] and the n-π* transition of the surface state [47,52], respectively. In addition, there is also a wide absorption peak between 365 and 600 nm, indicating that the NB-CDs can be efficiently excited at longer wavelengths to avoid the damage of excitation by UV light. When NB-CDs ethanol solution excited at 493 nm, the emission peak reaches the maximum at 597 nm. With UV light at 365 nm, the NB-CDs ethanol solution can exhibit strong orange photoluminescence immediately (Fig. 3a inset). The orange fluorescence is derived from the conjugated aromatic π domain because of the raw material p-PD with a benzene ring structure and nitrogen element, which is consistent with previous work [53]. In addition, the doping of boron favors the formation of hydrogen bonds, which will be more beneficial to the application of metal ion detection [42].

When the excitation wavelength is changed from 440 nm to 560 nm, the emission peak position of NB-CDs moves slightly, and changed around 597 nm, still showing orange fluorescence (Fig. 3b), which is beneficial to distinguish the metal ion detection of spectral from another in practical applications [54]. The influence of different concentrations of NB-CDs fluorescence intensity was also explored. As shown in Fig. S1, the optimal concentration of NB-CDs is 0.2 mg/mL, which is adopted in subsequent experiments. The QY of the NB-CDs was estimated to be 4.0%, and the fluorescence lifetime is 9.51 ns (Table S1). Moreover, the emission spectra properties of NB-CDs of 0.2 mg/mL in different solvents (water, N, N-dimethylformamide, ethanol, tetrahydrofuran, dichloromethane and toluene) were observed. As showed in Fig. S2, the polarity of solvent exhibits an obvious influence on the emission wavelength, which may be caused by dipole interactions between the CDs surface and solvent molecules, including H-bonding interaction [55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of Ce4+ ions based on NB-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain good sensitivity, the detection parameters including the response time, the molar ratio of boric acid to p-phenylenediamine, and mixture ratio of crude product and petroleum ether have been investigated and determined systematically. In order to evaluate the sensitivity of the fluorescent NB-CDs, luminescence responses were measured upon the addition of different amounts of Ce4+ (Fig. 4 a). It can be observed that the peak intensity at 597 nm significantly decreases with the addition of Ce4+ from 0 to 400 μM, indicating that Ce4+ can quench the emission of NB-CDs effectively. The correlations between the quenching effects (F0 / F) of the NB-CDs and the concentration of Ce4+ were provided. Fig. 4b shows a nice linear equation over the concentration range of 0–180 μM, giving F0 / F = 0.005X + 0.98. The detection limit is estimated to be 0.14 μM according to the 3σ/s rule. In particular, the color changes of NB-CDs solution could be observed under the chamber light and UV-light excitation at 365 nm according to the quenching degree of Ce4+ concentration (Fig. 4c-d). To our knowledge, the establishment of fluorescent sensing platform for Ce4+ detection based on CDs in long wavelength has never been reported. Table 1 shows the comparison of different fluorescent methods for Ce4+ detection, indicating our detecting system has better sensitivity and linear range than previously reported detection systems. In addition, the fluorescence sensing system prepared here is low-cost, simple operation, and environmentally friendly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratio fluorescence detection of Al3+ based on NB-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: NB-CDs are also utilized for fluorescence detection of Al3+. The effects of Al3+ concentrations on the emissions of NB-CDs were investigated systematically. Fig. 5 a offers the fluorescence emission spectra of NB-CDs solutions with different concentrations of Al3+. It can be found that the emission peaks of NB-CDs at 597 nm reduce dramatically as the concentration of Al3+ increases from 0 to 400 μM, whereas the emission peaks at 524 nm increase. Fig. 5b-c describes the relationship between Al3+ concentration and ratios of F524nm / F597nm in the range from 0 to 100 µmol·L−1, which is in accord with the following equations: Y = 0.00067X + 0.26313. The correlation coefficient is 0.9995. The detection limit for Al3+ by this method is about 1.07 μM (3σ/s), which is lower than the WHO standard of drinking water (7.41 µM) [57]. The result shows that NB-CDs can be used for the determination of Al3+ in drinking water. Fig. 5d-e show the color changes of the NB-CDs detection system to Al3+. It can be seen that the color of NB-CDs solution change from tan to light purple under chamber light, while their emission changes from orange to green under the excitation of 365 nm UV light with the addition of Al3+. The change of fluorescence intensity is accompanied with the change of fluorescence color, which is more reliable, advantageous and sensitive for visual detection of Al3+ than single fluorescence quenching method [58]. Compared with other detection CDs, the detection limit of this method is a little higher, but the linear range is wider, and the visualization of sensor Al3+ can be realized (Table 2 ). Therefore, the NB-CDs prepared here will be a valuable tool for the fluorescent visualization detection of Al3+ [59].

In order to further study the detection mechanism, the fluorescence response of pure nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) at different concentrations of Ce4+ and Al3+ was measured and recorded. As shown in Fig. 6 a and 6c, it clearly shows that under the action of different concentrations of Ce4+ or Al3+, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at 597 nm decreases continuously. The detection range of N-CDs on Ce4+ is linear within 0.96–120 μM (Fig. 6b), and the detection limit is about 0.96 μM, while the detection range of NB-CDs for Ce4+ is 0.14–180 μM, and the detection limit was about 0.14 μM (Fig. 4b), which indicating that the doping of B can improve the sensitivity of CDs to detection of Ce4+ and broaden the detection range. While for Al3+, the fluorescence behavior is irregular as the N-CDs are used for detection of Al3+, and only the fluorescence intensity decreases without wavelength shift (Fig. 6c and 6d). On the contrary, the fluorescence wavelength of NB-CDs changes from orange at 597 nm to green at 524 nm when detect Al3+, and the ratios of F524nm / F597nm in the range from 0 to 100 µmol·L−1 is linear regular (Fig. 5b-c). Moreover, fluorescence decay curves were also investigated to further study recognition mechanism of NB-CDs. As shown in the Fig. S3a and Table S1, fluorescence decay curves of the NB-CDs, NB-CDs with different concentration of Ce4+ and Al3+ all fit double-exponential function with the similar fluorescence lifetime about 9 ns, which illustrates the mechanism of static quenching from consistency of the fluorescence lifetime [42]. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of NB-CDs with Ce4+ (200 μM) appears three new peaks at 464 nm, 495 nm and a wide peak from 542 nm to 628 nm after the introduction of Ce4+, while a new wide peak between 490 nm and 660 nm is formed after the introduction of Al3+ compared with NB-CDs (Fig. S3b). Meanwhile, the original wide absorption peak of NB-CDs system between 365 and 600 nm almost disappear. Those may be attributed to the complex produced by the coordination of Ce4+ and NB-CDs, as well as Al3+ and NB-CDs.

Therefore, we conclude that the fluorescence quenching of NB-CDs is mainly caused by the interaction between the amino surface functional groups on CDs and metal ion of Ce4+ and Al3+ formed the complex, because of strong selective coordination between the N and Ce4+ or Al3+ [24,29]. It is interesting to note that the doping of B can enhance the coordination between N and Ce4+, making it easier for the coordination to occur, thus the detection sensitivity can be improved [63]. In addition, the boron atom has the corresponding electron-donating property, and forms a simple admixture (B → Al) in the absence of an active halide substituent on the borane, resulting in a blue shift of the emission properties [64,65]. Thus, the NB-CDs applied in detection of Ce4+ or Al3+, the effect is more obvious and the sensitivity is higher.

To investigate the specificity of the sensor system for the detection of Ce4+ and Al3+, the fluorescence of NB-CDs in the presence of some common metal ions (Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, K+, Li+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Hg2+) was recorded. As is shown in Fig. 7 a and S4a, except Fe3+, the fluorescence response of NB-CDs solution changed a little upon the addition of other ions, which could be ignored. However, Fe3+ can significantly reduce the fluorescence intensity of NB-CDs solution, which will affect the quantitative determination of Ce4+ and Al3+. Fortunately, the introduction of NaF or EDTA has little effect on the fluorescence response of NB-CDs solution, while the addition of NaF (Fig. S5a) and EDTA (Fig. S5b) can attenuate the effect of Fe3+ on the detection of Ce4+ and Al3+, respectively. Moreover, it is found that NaF can restore the fluorescence of NB-CDs and Al3+ from green to orange, which can be an effective strategy to determine them separately when Ce4+ and Al3+ coexist (Fig. S5c). In order to enhance the sensitivity and reproducibility of the fluorescence response of NB-CDs sensor, the effect of culture time on fluorescence intensity before and after adding Ce4+ and Al3+ into NB-CDs solution was studied. As shown in Fig. 7b-c and S4b-c, F / F0 and F527nm / F597nm are selectively cultured as a functions of incubation time, respectively. The results show that the fluorescence intensity of NB-CDs just shows only a slight decrease within 35 min probably because of the changes of the solvent due to the volatilization of ethanol. On the contrary, the fluorescence intensity decrease rapidly after Ce4+ or Al3+ is added into NB-CDs solution for 2 min, and can maintain this fluorescence properties for at least 35 min, which indicating that the synthesized probe has the ability to react quickly with Ce4+ and Al3+, and also has durability with the passage of time [66].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in the detection of Ce4+ and Al3+ in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate detection effect of proposed method in real samples, the real samples of tap and lake water were detected for Ce4+ and Al3+ through recovery tests. As showed in Table S2 and S3, it is illustrated that Ce4+and Al3+ are not detected in tap and lake water. Three samples were prepared by placing different concentrations of Ce4+ and Al3+ solutions into tap and lake water, respectively. After spiking with different concentrations of Ce4+ and Al3+, the recoveries of Ce4+ and Al3+ are all close to the results measured by reported fluorescent method [24,37]. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of Ce4+ and Al3+ is not exceeding 3% and 5%, respectively, which indicates that the probe has high accuracy for Ce4+ and Al3+ detection. All the above results demonstrate that the NB-CDs detection system has a good prospect in the analysis of Ce4+ and Al3+ in real samples.

In summary, a novel orange sensitive fluorescent sensor for Ce4+ and Al3+ detection has been developed by nitrogen and boron co-doped CDs (NB-CDs). The fluorescence of NB-CDs can be quenched by Ce4+ effectively and selectively, and the linear detection range of Ce4+ is gained from 0.14 to 180 μM with a detection limit as low as 0.14 μM. Furthermore, the linear detection range of Al3+ from 1.07 to 100 μM and a detection limit of as low as 1.07 μM are obtained, accompanied with a blue-shift to 527 nm. The NB-CDs show excellent detection capability towards Ce4+ and Al3+, and reaction process is economical, convenient and environmentally friendly, which have important practical application prospect.

Xin Li: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Liuxi Zhao: Investigation. Yuhan Wu: Formal analysis. Ao Zhou: Investigation. Xuanfeng Jiang: Investigation. Yuan Zhan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Zhengguang Sun: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Our work is financially supported by the Hubei Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2021CFB025) and the Oversea Expertise Introduction Center for Discipline Innovation (D18025).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121638.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The development of carbon dots: From the perspective of materials chemistry
Mater. Today

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Li, L. Li, H. Tu, H. Zhang, D.S. Silvester, C.E. Banks, G. Zou, H. Hou, X. Ji, The development of carbon dots: From the perspective of materials chemistry, Materials Today, 51 (2021) 188-207.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Functionalized carbon dots for advanced batteries
Energy Storage Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Guo, L. Li, B. Wang, Y. Xiang, G. Zou, Y. Zhu, H. Hou, X. Ji, Functionalized carbon dots for advanced batteries, Energy Storage Materials, 37 (2021) 8-39.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-pot synthesis of double silane-functionalized carbon dots with tunable emission and excellent coating properties for WLEDs application
Nanotechnology

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Cao, Y. Wu, X. Li, X. Jiang, Y. Zhang, Y. Zhan, Z. Sun, One-pot synthesis of double silane-functionalized carbon dots with tunable emission and excellent coating properties for WLEDs application, Nanotechnology, 33 (2021) 115703.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: Applications in bioimaging and theranostics
Int. J. Pharm.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.O. Boakye-Yiadom, S. Kesse, Y. Opoku-Damoah, M.S. Filli, M. Aquib, M.M.B. Joelle, M.A. Farooq, R. Mavlyanova, F. Raza, R. Bavi, B. Wang, Carbon dots: Applications in bioimaging and theranostics, International Journal of Pharmaceutics, 564 (2019) 308-317.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence mechanism of xylan-derived carbon dots: Toward investigation on excitation-related emission behaviors
J. Lumin.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Yang, Z. Zhu, W. Zhang, T. Zhang, X. Li, M. Luo, W. Chen, M. Chen, X. Zhou, Fluorescence mechanism of xylan-derived carbon dots: Toward investigation on excitation-related emission behaviors, Journal of Luminescence, 223 (2020) 117199.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Interfacing carbon dots for charge-transfer processes
Small

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Stergiou, N. Tagmatarchis, Interfacing carbon dots for charge-transfer processes, Small, 17 (2021) 2006005.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Comparison between hydrothermal and microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon dots from biowaste and chemical for heavy metal detection: A review
Microchem. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.K.M. Ng, G.K. Lim, C.P. Leo, Comparison between hydrothermal and microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon dots from biowaste and chemical for heavy metal detection: A review, Microchemical Journal, 165 (2021) 106116.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal ion detection by carbon dots—A review
Crit. Rev. Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Batool, H.M. Junaid, S. Tabassum, F. Kanwal, K. Abid, Z. Fatima, A.T. Shah, Metal ion detection by carbon dots—A review, Critical Reviews in Analytical Chemistry, (2020) 1-12.

Dyes Pigm.

Spectrochim. Acta Part A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.

Coord. Chem. Rev.

Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.
